text:
NHL



Team Canada has named Doug Armstrong general manager of its men’s ice hockey team for the 2026 Winter Olympics in Milan, Italy, the organization announced Friday.

Doug Armstrong has been named Hockey Canada's General Manager for the 2026 Winter Olympic games.#stlblues

MORE ➡️https://t.co/TeHP49JmYbhttps://t.co/TeHP49JmYb

— St. Louis Blues (@StLouisBlues)March 15, 2024



Armstrong, 59, will also “oversee the appointment of management groups that will lead Team Canada at various events over the next two seasons,” such as the 2024 IIHF World Championships and 2025 NHL 4 Nations Face-Off, an international tournament featuring players from Canada, U.S., Sweden and Finland.

Advertisement

He has a long and illustrious history with Team Canada in international competition. Armstrong served as an assistant on GM Steve Yzerman’s staff for the 2010 and 2014 Olympics in Vancouver and Sochi, respectively, winning gold medals at both tournaments. He was GM for the 2016 World Cup of Hockey and the 2023 IIHF World Championships, where the country also won gold.

Armstrong was scheduled to be in charge of Canada’s team at the 2022 Olympics in Beijing, but theNHLdecided not to participate and Armstrong was replaced by former NHL player Shane Doan.

In the NHL, Armstrong has been a GM of theDallas StarsandBlues. He was an assistant GM when the Stars won the Stanley Cup in 1999, and he was GM with St. Louis when it won the Cup in 2019.

He’s been the Blues’ GM since 2010 and has the third-most seniority in management across the league, trailing only Lou Lamoriello and Ken Holland. Recently, Armstrong won his 800th game as GM, becoming the second-fastest to reach the milestone (1,432 games) after Holland (1,381). He’s one of only 11 GMs to eclipse 800 wins and now trails No. 10 Bobby Clarke by just four wins.

Hockey Canada also announced Ryan Getzlaf will serve as player relations adviser. He had a 17-year playing career with theAnaheim Ducks(2005 to ’22), serving as captain for 12 seasons and winning the Stanley Cup in 2007. He appeared in 1,157 NHL games, posting 1,019 points (282 goals, 737 assists) and appearing in three NHL All-Star Games (2008, 2009, 2015). Internationally, he won gold medals at the 2010 and 2014 Olympics and the 2016 World Cup of Hockey.

If there’s a theme to how Hockey Canada has decided to approach the 2026 men’s Olympic tournament, it’s to try to find a common thread between the next two IIHF men’s world championships and the Four Nations event in the lead-up to theOlympics.

Advertisement

That’s why Olympic GM Doug Armstrong will help “oversee” the naming of GMs for men’s worlds and more importantly the Four Nations event but won’t be GM himself for those events.

My understanding is that the top candidates to be GM for Team Canada at the Four Nations event next year are Kelly McCrimmon, Don Sweeney, Julien BriseBois, Kevin Cheveldayoff and Jim Nill.

Again, Armstrong will be there to help advise in a support role for whoever gets the GM gig for that event.

The expectation is that Team Canada will name its GM for the Four Nations event this spring, likely before the Stanley Cup playoffs. So, soon enough.

It’s also important to note that whoever is named head coach for Team Canada at the Four Nations event won’t necessarily be guaranteed the gig for the Olympics. Those are two separate decisions because two different GMs are making those calls.

Jon Cooper was named head coach for Team Canada ahead of the 2022 Winter Games in China before the NHL pulled its participation. He would be a candidate again, among others.

The other thing to point out in this approach from Hockey Canada, which is really about using as many different NHL people as possible over the next few international events, is that players who are on the bubble for the 2026 Winter Olympic team probably will be well served to play in the IIHF world championships this spring and next to further beef up their international resume. Stanley Cup playoff performance will probably be the most important indicator, but playing in the men’s worlds will also be important if players are available to do so.— Pierre LeBrun, NHL senior writer

(Photo: Keith Gillett / Icon Sportswire via Getty Images)
